title: Your backup script hasn't run in days
problem: A nightly backup script at /opt/backup.sh was set up weeks ago by a colleague. The S3 uploads stopped appearing 3 days ago — nobody touched the script, the server is up, and cron is supposedly running. Figure out why cron stopped executing it and fix the crontab.

setup.description: Create the backup script and install a broken crontab entry that silently fails.
commands: sudo bash -c 'echo -e "#!/bin/bash\necho Backing up to S3... >> /var/log/backup.log" > /opt/backup.sh'
setup.note: The bug is subtle: the word 'root' in a personal crontab is treated as the command to run, not a username. That field only belongs in /etc/cron.d/ files.

hints: Start by checking that the cron daemon is actually running, then look at the system cron logs — they record every attempted execution.
Run crontab -l to inspect your crontab. Compare the format to the man page: five time fields, then directly the command. Anything extra before the command is suspicious.

The word 'root' in a user crontab is not a username — cron treats it as the command and tries to execute a binary called 'root', which does not exist. Remove it so the line reads: 0 2 * * * /opt/backup.sh >> /var/log/backup.log 2>&1
solution:
1. Check that cron is running:
   systemctl status cron
   # On RHEL/CentOS use: systemctl status crond

2. Look at cron logs for failures:
   grep CRON /var/log/syslog | tail -20
   # On RHEL/CentOS: tail -20 /var/log/cron
   # You will see entries like: (CRON) error (grandchild #N failed with exit status 127)
   # Exit status 127 means 'command not found'.

3. Inspect the current crontab:
   crontab -l
   # Output: 0 2 * * * root /opt/backup.sh >> /var/log/backup.log 2>&1
   # Spot the bug: 'root' is in position 6 — the command field.
   # Cron tried to run a binary called 'root'. It does not exist.

4. Fix the crontab:
   crontab -e
   # Change the line to:
   0 2 * * * /opt/backup.sh >> /var/log/backup.log 2>&1
   # Save and exit the editor.

5. Verify the fix:
   crontab -l
   # Should now show the corrected line without 'root'.

6. Test the script runs manually:
   bash /opt/backup.sh && tail /var/log/backup.log
   # Should print: Backing up to S3...
